data.title: Accenture's Strong Fundamentals Meet Slower Growth Outlook
data.description:
Accenture (NYSE:ACN) is in an interesting spot. The company just finished a strong fiscal 2025 with revenue up 7 percent and earnings up 8 percent. That shows it still has the scale and reach to grow even in a tougher environment. At the same time, the outlook for fiscal 2026 is weaker, with management guiding for only 2 to 5 percent growth. Slower demand from government clients is part of the problem and it means the next year could feel like a pause.

The stock trades around 17 times forward earnings. That feels about right for a business that is reliable but not showing big growth right now. After a 33 percent drop over the past year, the shares are sitting near their low, which tells me the market has already priced in much of the short-term risk. In my view, Accenture is a solid company that pays investors well but the near-term growth story is not strong enough to call it a buy.

A Diversified Giant Investing in AI and Acquisitions

Accenture is the one of the largest players in consulting and IT services, with close to $70 billion in annual revenue. It covers everything from advising on strategy to building software and running day-to-day operations. With about 779,000 employees across the world, it has the scale to serve big clients in almost every industry.

The company is pushing harder into artificial intelligence and digital services. In 2025, it brought in almost $6 billion in bookings tied to AI. It also bought several smaller companies to boost its strength in areas like cybersecurity, Salesforce services and marketing. These moves keep it up to date with what clients are asking for. The long-term setup looks good but it usually takes time for acquisitions and new offerings to show up clearly in revenue.

Industry Shifts Create Opportunity but Also Doubt

The consulting and tech services field is shifting quickly. Clients want help with cloud systems, data and AI. That creates opportunity but it also creates doubt. There are questions about whether firms like Accenture can really deliver the results they are promising when it comes to AI. At the same time, budget pressure in areas like U.S. federal work is weighing on growth.

Accenture’s advantage is that it can handle the whole job, from advice to execution to ongoing support. Few rivals have that full package. Still, competition is intense from Indian IT firms, from the big cloud providers and from niche AI specialists. That means Accenture must prove that its size and experience actually translate into better results for clients.

Financial Strength Paired With Signs of Slower Demand

In 2025, Accenture delivered nearly $70 billion in sales and almost $13 in adjusted earnings per share. Margins stayed steady and free cash flow jumped to about $11 billion. This kind of cash flow gives the company plenty of flexibility to return money to investors or fund new deals.

Looking deeper, performance was mixed across areas. Financial services clients spent more, while public service work declined slightly in Q4 but still grew for the full year. Bookings were slightly lower than last year, which hints that demand is leveling off. The balance sheet is in good shape with more than $11 billion in cash and about $8 billion in total debt. So while growth is slowing, the financial foundation is still very strong.

Fair Value Already Reflects Current Outlook

At around $239 a share, Accenture trades at roughly 16.8 times forward earnings and about 12.3 times EV to EBITDA. Those numbers put it above Cognizant (CTSH), which sits near 13 times earnings and just over 8 times EV to EBITDA and a step below Infosys (INFY), which trades above 20 times earnings and around 16 times EV to EBITDA.

Historically, Accenture has traded higher when growth was stronger. Now that the multiple has slipped into the mid-teens, it is clear that investors expect more modest results ahead, especially with management calling for only 2 to 5 percent revenue growth in fiscal 2026.

Analysts expect earnings to climb to almost $14 in 2026 and about $15 in 2027. That suggests steady, mid-single-digit growth. By those numbers, the fair value looks to be in the 230 to 260 range. With the stock already near the bottom of that range, there is not much downside but there is not a lot of near-term upside either. Upside depends on whether demand picks up or artificial intelligence work converts into more meaningful revenue, while downside is cushioned by stable earnings and cash flow.

Fragile Market Sentiment Despite Dividend Increase

Right now, investor mood around Accenture is cautious. The shares have fallen sharply over the past year, reflecting concern about slowing growth and questions about how fast AI will actually add to revenue. Analysts have responded similarly, with moves like Stifel’s price target cut.

Even so, there are positives. The company beat expectations last quarter and announced a 10 percent dividend hike, showing that leadership remains confident in the long-term story. On the flip side, headlines about AI layoffs create confusion, since they clash with the idea of Accenture leading the AI charge. Overall, sentiment feels fragile but it may also be too negative compared to the company’s actual strength.

Risks From Slower Spending, AI Skepticism and Workforce Changes

The biggest near-term risk is that growth slows more than expected. Guidance already points to only a few percent of revenue growth in 2026 and that could slip if client budgets stay tight. Another risk is that AI ends up being overhyped and projects fail to deliver the value that clients expect. If that happens, demand could take a hit.

Government spending cuts are another headwind and since Accenture does significant work with public sector clients, that matters. Acquisitions also carry integration risks and the company is spending heavily to reshape its workforce, which led to hundreds of millions in severance costs last year. All of these factors are worth watching closely, although Accenture’s strong financials provide a cushion if things go wrong.

A Transition Year Before Longer-Term Growth Picks Up

Looking to the next year, management expects earnings to grow about 5 to 8 percent. That is healthy but not at the pace Accenture has delivered in the past. Longer term, forecasts show revenue climbing toward $83 billion by 2028, with earnings moving above $16 a share.

The wild card is AI. If Accenture can turn its AI bookings into meaningful, repeatable revenue, the story gets more exciting. If not, then the company may only deliver modest growth until the next wave of demand arrives. For now, the picture looks steady but not thrilling, which keeps the stock in hold territory.

Scenarios Range From Soft Demand to AI-Driven Upside

The downside case is that growth slips to the low end of guidance, bookings stay soft and AI projects disappoint. In that case, earnings might only grow a few percent and the stock could fall toward $200. The base case, which I see as most likely, is that Accenture grows in the low single digits next year and picks up slightly after that. That would mean steady earnings growth of about 7 to 8 percent a year and the stock would likely trade between 230 and 260, with dividends adding stability.

The best case is that AI adoption really kicks in, government spending pressure fades and bookings bounce back. If that happens, earnings could climb to $17 by 2028 and the stock could return to the mid-300s. At this point, all three outcomes feel possible but the base case fits best with the current picture.

Final Take

To me, Accenture looks like a stock to hold rather than buy or sell. It is still one of the strongest companies in its space, with dependable cash flow, a strong balance sheet and consistent shareholder payouts. But growth is slowing and the market is not ready to reward the shares with a higher valuation until that changes.

At today’s price, the stock looks fairly valued for the performance it is likely to deliver over the next couple of years. For long-term investors, it makes sense to stay patient and keep holding. For new buyers, waiting for clearer signs of growth or a better entry point might be the smarter move. My bottom line is that Accenture is too solid to sell but not compelling enough to buy right now.

data.title: If I Could Retire On Only 5 Investments, It Would Be These
data.description:
Written by Austin Rogers for High Yield Investor

Let me pose a seemingly unlikely scenario to you.

Let's say you have $1 million and want to retire today. Perhaps you just received an inheritance, or maybe you have a $1 million 401K portfolio invested in broadly diversified and growth-heavy stocks like the S&P 500 (SPY) and Nasdaq Index (QQQ).

Whatever the particular details, you have enough saved and want to retire so as to begin the next phase of your life while living off your nest egg.

Okay, how do you do it?

The standard advice is to follow the 4% rule: Withdraw 4% of your portfolio value every year in whatever temporal increments (monthly, quarterly, biannually, etc.) that work best for you, and then adjust for inflation annually. Historically, the stock market has appreciated by an average of nearly 10% per year, which means that your portfolio should theoretically last indefinitely under the 4% rule.

However, many retirees find the bare-bones 4% rule rather dissatisfying. It requires selling shares, which certainly feels like slowly depleting one's portfolio even if stock appreciation does make this indefinitely viable. It also requires one to keep selling shares even during a bear market, which depletes one's portfolio even faster and can be anxiety-inducing.

With stock market valuations as elevated as they are today, it would be especially nerve-wracking to retire today on the assumption that broad indices like SPY and QQQ will continue to appreciate without a significant pullback in the near future.

Lots of investors, myself included, are drawn to the alternative retirement strategy of generating sufficient dividend income to live solely off that passive income without needing to sell shares.

This is the equivalent of living off of the fruit of the tree rather than incrementally lopping off fruit-bearing limbs to use for firewood. (Maybe this isn't a perfect analogy, but it communicates the idea.)

This income-focused method prioritizes three goals. In ascending order, they are:

  1. Maximize safe, current income
  2. Grow income at an inflation-matching or -beating rate
  3. Grow the portfolio value over time via price appreciation and (if possible) dividend reinvestment

When I retire someday, these will be my priorities.

Putting myself in the position of a retiree or near-retiree, how would I implement this strategy in a simple, low-maintenance, easy to construct and easy to manage portfolio?

That leads to the 5-fund portfolio below.

A Simple, 5-Fund Portfolio To Retire On

Remember: We're going for simplicity and low-maintenance here. We want a portfolio that, in theory, we would never need to touch again after the initial investment. I don't know how many do-it-yourself retirees would opt for this level of simplicity, but let's engage in the exercise anyway.

Were I to build a 5-investment portfolio from scratch in order to retire today, here's what it would be:

  • Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD)
  • Global X MLP & Energy Infrastructure ETF (MLPX)
  • Fundamental Income Net Lease ETF (NETL)
  • Reaves Utility Income Fund (UTG)
  • Goldman Sachs Nasdaq 100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ)

SCHD owns a basket of 100 dividend growth stocks with relatively high dividend yields. The ETF currently yields 3.7%, which is an indicator of the general range of dividend yielders it owns. While its largest sectoral weightings vary over time, it consistently has little to no exposure to utilities and real estate. This ETF has a remarkably strong record of dividend growth considering its relatively high yield.

Next, MLPX owns a concentrated (25-30 stocks) portfolio of midstream energy companies. These businesses own and operate oil & gas pipelines, storage facilities, processing plants, and export terminals. They don't produce oil & gas like upstream players, nor do they sell oil & gas to consumers like the downstream players do. They operate in the middle of the energy space. And since their assets tend to be contracted for long periods of time at fixed rates, their cash flows are far more stable than for upstream energy companies. That stability is appealing to retirees.

For real estate exposure, I'd argue that the net lease REITs owned by NETL are a great choice. Net leases are long-term, fixed-rent contracts that put the tenant in charge of all or most property expenses, such as building maintenance, insurance, and taxes. This tends to result in highly stable cash flows and dividends, which are beneficial for retirees.

Next up, for utilities exposure, I think UTG is a phenomenal option for retirees. This is a closed-end fund ("CEF") that uses 20-30% leverage to generate a larger income stream from its actively managed, utility-heavy portfolio. While this leverage naturally makes some nervous, it should be noted that the portfolio managers at Reaves Asset Management have accomplished the impressive feat of growing or maintaining UTG's monthly distribution for over 20 consecutive years. To be sure, the distribution has grown at only a low- to mid-single-digit pace in the years that it has grown, but few other CEFs could boast the record UTG can.

Finally, for exposure to the technology sector, I favor GPIQ, which is managed by Goldman Sachs for a mere 0.29% expense ratio. The ETF operates an options-writing strategy in order to generate premium income to support its high yield. When the tech stocks in the Nasdaq index are rising, GPIQ performs well and increases its dividend payout. But when tech stocks decline in price, both GPIQ's share price and its dividend decline as well.

Since the inception of GPIQ, the most recent of these five funds to debut on the public markets, this 5-fund portfolio has generated a wide range of total returns:

To be fair, though, retirees who own these five funds would withdraw all or most dividends, so total returns would scarcely matter.

Here's how the portfolio has performed on a price basis alone.

If you owned these five funds with equal weighting (20% of your portfolio in each), the price appreciation of the total portfolio would have been about 36% since the launch of GPIQ in late 2023. Comparing that to the SPY's price appreciation of ~60% since then, we find that this portfolio does underperform the market by quite a bit.

But remember: Our #1 goal is to maximize safe, current income. Our #2 goal is to grow that income at a rate that matches or exceeds the rate of inflation over time.

Capital appreciation is only a tertiary goal, since we don't intend to sell shares in this portfolio anyway.

This simple, equal-weighted portfolio has a dividend yield of 5.87%, which means that if one used $1 million in cash to construct this portfolio today, it would render an annual $58,700 in dividend income.

Here's how the portfolio breaks down by share of total income generated and the general level of expected dividend growth:

ETF Weight % of Total Income Dividend Growth
SCHD 20% 13% Inflation-Beating
MLPX 20% 16% Inflation-Matching or Beating
NETL 20% 16% Inflation-Matching or Beating
UTG 20% 21% Flat to Low Growth
GPIQ 20% 33% Fluctuating

As you can see, GPIQ accounts for 33% of income despite commanding only a 20% weight in the portfolio. All else being equal, that would lead me to minimize its weight in my portfolio and find other, more stable sources of high-yielding income. But for the sake of this exercise, I'm trying to keep it as simple as possible.

Even within this equal-weight portfolio, it should be noted that dividend growth from SCHD, MLPX, and NETL should, over time, offset fluctuations in GPIQ.

A retiree utilizing this portfolio would do well to count on (for living expenses) only 60-70% of the current income so as not to be caught flat-footed by a tech selloff that drags down GPIQ's payout.

Now, let's take a look at the 5-fund portfolio's weighted average sector exposure:

Sector Weighted Average Portfolio Exposure
Energy 26% (Midstream = 20%)
Real Estate 21%
Technology 13%
Utilities 12%
Communications 7%
Cons. Discretionary 5%
Cons. Staples 4%
Industrials 4%
Healthcare 4%
Financials 2%

On the surface, 26% exposure to the energy sector sounds way too aggressive. But keep in mind that 20 out of those 26 percentage points are in the midstream space, which, as explained above, is generally more conservative and defensive than the upstream space.

Real estate's share at 21% is also on the high side, but again, keep in mind that 20 percentage points of that is in net lease REITs that tend to exhibit more stable cash flows than the broader real estate sector.

Having less than 5% of the portfolio in consumer staples, industrials, healthcare, and financials sectors does strike me as a problem. Diversification is generally prudent for investors and especially for retirees.

But modifying the portfolio to increase exposure to these sectors would require either (1) reducing the overall portfolio yield, (2) increasing the use of CEFs, or (3) increasing the use of options ETFs. None of these choices strike me as particularly attractive. The first goes against goal #1 above. The other two ratchet up the use of complex financial vehicles (leverage or options-writing) and thereby add layers of risk.

Bottom Line

I don't claim that the 5-fund portfolio presented above is a perfect one for retirees seeking to follow an income-focused strategy. I don't know what a perfect portfolio would be, and I doubt anyone else knows either.

But the above portfolio would work to meet the three goals explained above, in my view, and it's what I would do if I wanted an ultra-simple, low-maintenance income portfolio.

It maximizes income without overindulging in highly risky assets or financial instruments. It gives ample exposure to dividend growth stocks that should naturally increase portfolio income over time. And it also appreciates in value over time, even if not at nearly the rate that the SPY does.

data.title: JIVE Vs. The World: The New Standard In International Value ETFs
data.description:
The Jpmorgan International Value ETF (NASDAQ:JIVE) is a relatively new active international value play that promises to do better than peers because of a higher diversification bias, including decent and agile exposure to emerging markets. In this thesis, we will evaluate how its methodology structurally positions it for material outperformance, while keeping risks (or drawdown profiles) unchanged.

Active Methodology with Value Focus Only

JIVE invests in value equity securities of both developed ex-US and emerging markets using a framework of quantitative and behavioral based selection process. The methodology also takes into account ESG factors. Currency exposures are actively managed. The focus is to identify valuation opportunities through the quantitative and behavioral selection model. Fundamental analysis based on proprietary data, internal research and analysis is used as an input to the selection and portfolio weighting process. There is no bias on market caps, so investors can expect concentration in different market cap types at different points of time. From the methodology description, JIVE appears to be agile across geographies (including emerging markets), market caps and sectors. The portfolio and its currency exposures are actively managed.

Portfolio Characteristics

The portfolio consists of ~340 holdings with the Top 10 accounting for less than 14% of the overall portfolio. The top weight is only 1.69% in Roche Holding. This is a very well diversified portfolio for its size. The portfolio direction is not determined primarily by a single stock or a group of concentrated holdings, allowing a generic value theme to take shape more consistently over different market conditions.

The top 10 names also show how the active and unique proprietary quantitative and behavioral model leads to picks that are generally not the star cast in most international ETFS. For example, high weights assigned to Mitsubishi UFJ Financial is not usually seen in most international ETFs with greater focus on consumer staples or technology. Shell and TotalEnergies are also often not seen in the top 10 of broader international ETFs, unless the emphasis is on energy focus or value orientation. The active methodology does not come up with a completely unexpected list, but does show enough active bets to carry decent alpha generation potential.

The sectoral allocation is broadly on expected lines with an expected emphasis on financials and a very low exposure to tech. This is very similar to the sectoral allocation of another international value ETF, EFV. There are some differences in JIVE, such as a notable underweight in consumer staples, utilities and healthcare. JIVE is also overweight in tech and consumer discretionary.

The regional diversification is quite good for an international ETF. Where most international ETFs are heavily invested in UK/EMEA. EFV has around 68% exposure in EMEA and 21% in Japan. JIVE takes out allocations from both these heavy weight regions to invest a significant ~24% in Asia ex-Japan. That should bring in more emerging markets into play than a typical international ETF. The Asia emphasis ends up with decent exposure to China, South Korea and Hong Kong. India too gets a 2% exposure, although not showing up in the top 10 - emphasizing decent emerging markets allocation.

The active nature of the fund is reflected in how the allocations change relatively quickly compared to how passive funds evolve over time. Below is a snapshot of the country wise allocations in October 2024. Within less than a year, China allocations have almost halved and UK allocation has gone up by 3-4 percentage points, for example. Overall agility in the geographical allocations is likely to be reflected in terms of markets cap, sectors and stocks as well. All in all, expect JIVE to be a diversified portfolio by market cap, geography and stock picks. The sectoral allocation is broadly in line with what we see in other international ETFs, not by design but because value portfolios often lead to such financials heavy weights. Also expect JIVE to be very agile and needing periodic reviewing for portfolio and performance changes.

Performance Comparisons and What to Expect

A peek into JIVE's total return profile should reveal the expected resilience of its more diversified portfolio. The active management's alpha contribution should also be apparent one way or the other. JIVE's total returns since its inception in around Sep 2023, against a peer set of international value ETFs shows how the rest closely trace each other, while JIVE stands out by a margin. The growth profile is consistently standout and by a margin of 12 percentage points where the peer set has gained around ~45% on average.

The higher growth could be attributed both to the active strategy as well as its higher emerging market mix in the portfolio. Since it has a higher smaller cap exposure compared to peers, that too is growth conducive, particularly in emerging markets. JIVE looks all set to be a standout growth conducive value play among international value alternatives. But does it mitigate drawdowns better due to its diversity? The answer there is also affirmative. In the tariff sell-off, which is the worst drawdown since JIVE's inception, we see average drawdown levels in line with peers - nothing extraordinary, potentially a result of the diversification across stocks and geographies if not market caps.

Wins on All Counts

JIVE wins on all counts. Its diversification helps manage drawdowns as well as most of the more conservative peers like EFV, DFIV and IVLU. The value strategy's defensive behavior is at par with alternative strategies. And then comes the explosive upside potential we have seen so far. JIVE grows extremely well for a value ETF, leaving its peers behind - gaining from its emerging market and smaller cap exposure (as expected from its methodology that does not look at market cap as a variable)

JIVE is a Buy for international value seekers because it retains the defensiveness of the value spectrum while delivering crucial alpha consistently. The macroeconomic scenario, going ahead, could be uncertain for emerging markets, where JIVE potentially derives much of its performance from. However, for long term investors, even a reduced outperformance for a few quarters will eventually be overshadowed by total return outperformance, if emerging markets remain under pressure for a while. The other factors like stock diversification, active management and market cap diversification should still help the performance till emerging market outperformance returns.

data.title: Plains GP: Business Expansion A Path To Increased Profits
data.description:
Investment Thesis

My main investment idea for September 2025 is to acquire assets in the oil and gas industry, which is logical given the expected recovery of the oil and natural gas market. In a previous article, I recommended buying shares in Schlumberger Limited, a company that is one of the leaders in the oil production and oil drilling solutions industry. But now I'm wondering about finding a company that deals with storage and delivery from producers to customers.

While benchmarking the oil and gas transportation industry, I noticed Plains GP Holdings (NASDAQ:PAGP). This is a company that has had 4 out of 5 analyst forecast revisions to the upside for Plains GP Holdings' earnings per share [EPS] over the past month. The main reason for these revisions is the company's reorganization. It expects to close a $3.75 billion deal to sell its loss-making gas business and instead acquire high-quality assets that will expand its oil business. After conducting a fundamental analysis and fair value modeling of the company, I found that its investment potential for share growth is at least 20% per annum. Therefore, based on the evidence described below in my article, I recommend PAGP shares as a “buy.”

Company Overview

Plains GP Holdings is an integrated infrastructure organization that provides crude oil and liquefied natural gas delivery services to key demand centers. The geographical scope of the company's operations is concentrated in the United States and Canada. Plains GP Holdings' business idea is linked to the need to participate in ensuring the efficient and secure supply of energy resources to North America. This is revealed in their comprehensive offerings to customers, meeting incoming requests to satisfy the demand for crude oil and gas condensate. Plains GP Holdings employs approximately 4,000 people, enabling it to deliver 8 million barrels of crude oil per day.

The company's competitive advantage is its leading position in the US oil and gas pipeline infrastructure market. The 25-year history of Plains GP Holdings has enabled it to build and operate more than 20,000 miles of pipelines for the delivery of crude oil and natural gas condensate. Storage facilities with a capacity of 72 million barrels have been built to ensure storage. This, at a minimum, allows for full utilization of storage facilities for 9 days of full capacity (based on crude oil supply of 8 million barrels per day). The company's transport fleet also consists of 2,300 trucks and 5,400 rail tank cars. Plains GP Holdings therefore provides full-cycle hydrocarbon delivery services: from loading at the point of extraction, pipeline delivery to logistics centers, and distribution of oil and gas between transport vehicles, delivering energy resources to the end customer.

Plains GP Holdings Financial Profile: Sale Of Loss-Making Gas Business And Expansion Of Oil Assets

The results of the report for the second quarter of 2025 came as a surprise to investors. The company reported GAAP earnings per share of $0.05, which is $0.22 less than analysts' forecasts. Revenue was $10.64 billion, which is $2.11 billion lower than forecast. Regardless, it pleased shareholders with the following positive changes. First, the decision to sell the entire liquefied natural gas business. The deal is worth $3.75 billion and will simplify Plains GP Holdings' existing business model. The reasoning behind the sale of the gas business can be found in the second quarter report, which shows a breakdown of adjusted EBITDA by product. Adjusted EBITDA from natural gas in the second quarter of 2025 decreased by 7% compared to the same period in 2024. Meanwhile, the majority of Plains GP Holdings' profits come from the development of its oil business.

More than $3 billion of the proceeds from the sale will be used to repurchase preferred shares and pursue new merger and acquisition opportunities. On July 22, 2025, an additional 20% of BridgeTex Pipeline Company shares were acquired, increasing the existing stake to 40%.

Additionally, on September 2, 2025, Plains GP Holdings announced the acquisition of a 55% stake in EPIC Crude Holdings. The transaction amounted to $1.57 billion. At the same time, it may increase by $193 million if the achievement of pipeline capacity increase targets from 600,000 barrels per day to 900,000 barrels per day. This acquisition will enable the delivery of crude oil from the Permian Basin and Eagle Ford basins to the Gulf Coast market in Corpus Christi. The assets acquired will enable Plains GP Holdings to increase its operating capacity by more than 600,000 barrels per day, of which 200,000 barrels will be for export. It will also increase its crude oil storage capacity by 7 million barrels. Another advantage is the 800-mile increase in the length of its trunk pipelines.

Following the August 8, 2025, conference call, investors were informed that the company's 2025 earnings guidance remains unchanged (this includes a weaker Q2 2025 performance). Plains GP Holdings expects adjusted EBITDA in the range of $2.8 billion to $2.85 billion for 2025. This current guidance remains valid due to increased production in the Permian Basin, which supports a stable flow of orders from customers for the storage and transportation of crude oil. Detailed information on the 2025 guidance is provided below. The majority of revenue will continue to come from the oil business, with EBITDA expected to be $2.41 billion, while the gas business is expected to contribute $0.45 billion. Following the implementation of this business profile, EBITDA is expected to increase in the first quarter of 2026 from the acquisition of new assets in the crude oil business.

Risks Of Plains GP Holdings

Despite the positive assessment of the investment attractiveness of Plains GP Holdings, their business model is subject to the negative impact of a number of risks. First, there are risks associated with high price volatility in the crude oil and liquefied natural gas market. Management has confirmed that initial expectations for market recovery were overstated compared to the current assessment. However, volatility risks remain relevant in the short term (until the end of 2025), while long-term indicators reflect confidence in market recovery.

Some other risks that Plains GP Holdings shareholders are keeping an eye on are execution risks related to capital reallocation, contract rollovers, and the possibility of financing additional risks. But closing the deal to sell the gas business in Q1 2026 will boost the company's revenue by $3.75 billion. Approximately $3 billion from this transaction will be used to provide additional financing for investment projects and the acquisition of new oil assets. Therefore, this risk is temporary and carries less damage than the threat of continued market volatility in crude oil prices.

PAGP Stock Forecast And Targets

To determine the target value of PAGP shares, I conducted financial modeling using DCF. This model allows us to identify the fair value of the company. The modeling will yield comparative results, which will allow us to answer the question of Plains GP Holdings' investment attractiveness. For the forecast part of the DCF model, I chose the nearest 5-year period, from 2026 to 2030. The main valuation tool is the discount rate. In my calculations, I used the WACC rate, which is approximately 7.75% (calculated based on the cost of equity). Another tool of the DCF model is EPS, which for the company is $0.05 for Q2 2025. The expected profit growth for Plains GP Holdings in the forecast period is 9.5%.

This increase can be justified by the expected completion of the company's restructuring. By the beginning of 2026, the sale of the liquefied natural gas business is expected to be completed, which will allow Plains GP Holdings to focus on developing its oil business. The sale of the liquefied natural gas business is expected to be completed by early 2026, allowing Plains GP Holdings to focus on developing its oil business. This will significantly simplify the business model, reducing its exposure to natural gas price volatility risks. In addition, the restructuring of the company will improve financial flexibility, allowing it to find additional investments for more profitable projects.

An additional factor contributing to the investment attractiveness of PAGP shares is the external fundamental background. First, this is due to the recovery of the oil market, which had previously been in a bearish trend until 2025. From April to September 2025, Brent crude oil prices rose from $58 to $68 per barrel. The price formed a bullish trend channel on the daily chart, signaling a potential recovery of the oil market to $77.76 (key resistance line) and $82.50 (upper trend channel boundary).

In the second place, the geopolitical situation around the world is expected to improve. This is due to the current US foreign policy, which allows for the resolution of many regional conflicts. At the same time, economic growth is recovering. The latest quarterly GDP growth data for the second quarter of 2025 in the US showed record values of +3.8% (instead of the forecast +3.3%). These are the highest values since the third quarter of 2023. In addition, the September report of the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD) raised its forecast for global GDP growth in 2025 from +2.9% to +3.2%. These macroeconomic conditions create the potential for increased demand in the global oil and petroleum products market, which should have a positive impact on the shares of companies involved in oil production, oil transportation, and petroleum product manufacturing.

The DCF model uses free cash flow as the key indicator for assessing the fair value of Plains GP Holdings. Taking into account the use of borrowed funds, this figure amounted to $979.8 million by the end of 2025. Without the use of borrowed funds, this indicator is $1.218 billion. However, for a more accurate forecast of the company's fair value, it is worth deducting the use of borrowed funds. Therefore, I took the indicator of $979.8 million.
